## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, third party data validates you and your presentation. The rule to handle buyer distrust, to build credibility, is always use and show written material to support your presentation and your proposal. When you're documenting facts for your customer, it's preferable to use third party materials rather than something from you, right? So if Bob said it about this product, that's more believable than if I said it. If my manufacturer said it and that was supported by maybe some other third parties, that would be more acceptable to the consumer. Third party validation. Remember, people believe what they see, not what they hear, and always, always write down what you've said, offered, proposed, promised, implied, or suggested, right? And anytime you can use third party data, use it, okay? Anytime you're going for the close, insist on that it's in writing. You don't want to shy away from contracts, buyers' orders, and signatures. hey, I can do this with this guy. I believe in this guy. Have the data. I don't need to shop. Why do people shop? Distrust. Grand, people trust me. ## [06:03 - 07:28]

You hear that? Your customers are getting emotional, you know what? Data, logic is missing. And when people get emotional, they get irrational. And when people get irrational, they do dumb things. So it's okay to sell with emotion. You want people emotional when they're buying, when they're getting involved in the product, but you want them to close, to be rational in the close.
